data.source: In-person laboratory experiments with recruited subjects (students and others) at University of Michigan and Temple University; experimental choices (incentive-compatible monetary choices) elicited in-session.
data.wave_or_subset: Two main experiments (Experiment 1 and Experiment 2); Experiment 2 pooled subjects from UMich and Temple and reports subgroup analyses (native blacks, immigrant blacks, whites, men, women).
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual subject (choices clustered by subject; analyses pool subjects' multiple choices and cluster standard errors by subject).
data.notes: Payment implemented by randomly selecting one choice for payment; delayed payments via post-dated checks. Between 35% and 53% of participants expressed disbelief in the payment structure (skeptical subjects), and several robustness checks exclude these skeptical subjects. Dependent variables are interval-censored (finite set of binary choices) requiring interval regression on transformed latent variables.

method.description: Randomized laboratory experiments in which social identities (ethnic, racial, or gender) were made salient via questionnaire priming before eliciting subjects' time and risk preferences. Time preferences were elicited via multiple binary intertemporal choices (smaller-sooner vs larger-later) with half comparing immediate vs one week and half one week vs two weeks. Risk preferences were elicited via multiple binary choices between a certain smaller amount and a larger lottery with varying probability. One of a subject's choices was randomly selected and paid (delayed payments via post-dated checks). Econometric analysis used interval regression on transformed latent dependent variables (log continuously compounded reservation interest rate for time choices; reservation risk premium for risk choices), with clustered Huber-White standard errors by subject.
method.steps: 1) Recruit subjects at two locations (University of Michigan and Temple University). 2) Randomly assign subjects to control or identity-salience treatment (ethnicity, race, or gender made salient via questionnaire items). 3) Elicit time preference choices: 46 binary intertemporal decisions (half immediate vs 1 week; half 1 week vs 2 weeks). 4) Elicit risk preference choices: 18 binary choices between a certain $4 and an $8 gamble with varying success probabilities. 5) Randomly select one choice per subject for real payment; use post-dated checks for delayed payments. 6) Transform reservation gross interest rates using continuous compounding and take logs; compute reservation risk premium. 7) Estimate interval regression models on latent dependent variables, clustering standard errors by subject and including covariates (treatment indicator, tradeoff type, recruitment location, stake size, and interactions). 8) Conduct robustness analyses excluding skeptical subjects who doubted payment credibility.
method.models: Interval regression on interval-censored latent dependent variables (log continuously compounded reservation interest rate for intertemporal choices; reservation risk premium for risky choices). Regressors include Social Category Salient indicator and controls (tradeoff type, Larger Stakes indicator, UMich recruitment indicator, interactions). Huber-White standard errors clustered by subject reported.
method.outcome_variable: Log of the continuously compounded reservation interest rate (time preference task) and the reservation risk premium (risk preference task).
method.independent_variables: Primary treatment indicator: Social Category Salient (race, ethnicity, or gender salience). Other explanatory variables include tradeoff type indicators (Immediate vs 1 week; 1 week vs 2 weeks), Larger Stakes indicator for risky choices, recruitment location (UMich), and interactions between tradeoff type and recruitment location.
method.control_variables: 1 Week vs 2 Weeks indicator (for time choices), Larger Stakes indicator (for risk choices), UMich recruitment indicator, and interaction terms; demographic subgroup indicators (native black, immigrant black, white, male, female) used in subgroup analyses.

results.summary: Making ethnic or racial identity salient significantly affected intertemporal choices for certain groups but had mixed or limited effects on risk preferences. In Experiment 1, Asian subjects primed with ethnicity chose substantially fewer impatient options (i.e., became more patient). In Experiment 2, making race salient reduced measured discount rates among native (non-immigrant) blacks but had no effect among immigrant blacks; whites generally showed no consistent effect except in some restricted samples of subjects who believed they would be paid. Gender salience produced no robust effects. Risk preferences: priming race increased measured risk aversion among native blacks in several specifications (potentially explaining lower stock market participation), while overall effects of priming on risk attitudes across groups were weaker and less consistent. Excluding skeptical subjects altered several estimated effects, indicating sensitivity to beliefs about payment credibility.

numerical_results.p_value: not stated (reported as statistically significant in paper)
not stated (reported as significant for native blacks in paper)

numerical_results.effect_size: coefficient estimate in restricted (believers) sample at Temple
